heuristics.rule: If you are India-born or China-born and pursuing EB-2, treat the priority date backlog — not your qualifications — as the primary planning variable.
heuristics.condition: When an Indian or Chinese national begins planning a US green card pathway
heuristics.action: Check the Visa Bulletin immediately and calculate your realistic wait time under per-country quotas before committing to a US long-term career strategy
heuristics.because: Per-country quotas cap each nation at 7% of annual EB visas regardless of demand — India and China face 50-100+ year backlogs under current law, making qualifications nearly irrelevant to timing

heuristics.rule: If you qualify for NIW, file it as a self-petition rather than waiting for employer PERM sponsorship — it saves approximately 1 year of process time.
heuristics.condition: When a STEM PhD, healthcare professional, or entrepreneur can demonstrate substantial merit and national importance to the US
heuristics.action: Pursue the NIW track to bypass PERM labor certification entirely
heuristics.because: PERM alone takes approximately 1 year (6 months recruitment + ~158 days DOL processing), and NIW removes both the employer dependency and that entire timeline segment

heuristics.rule: Use I-140 premium processing when employer timeline pressure or personal status uncertainty exists — the $2,805 fee buys a decision in 15 business days vs. 8+ months.
heuristics.condition: When an H-1B holder's status is nearing expiration or an employer needs certainty before extending a job offer
heuristics.action: File Form I-907 alongside I-140 to elect premium processing
heuristics.because: Standard I-140 processing averages 8.1 months as of 2025; delays in I-140 approval delay everything downstream including AC21 protections and 3-year H-1B extensions

heuristics.rule: If you are an India-born EB-2 holder and EB-3 priority dates are advancing faster than EB-2, consult an attorney immediately about a strategic EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade.
heuristics.condition: When the monthly Visa Bulletin shows EB-3 India dates moving faster than EB-2 India dates
heuristics.action: Request an attorney assessment of filing a concurrent or downgrade I-140 under EB-3 to capture the earlier priority date movement
heuristics.because: The EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade is a legal strategy that can accelerate green card issuance by years if EB-3 retrogression is temporarily less severe than EB-2

heuristics.rule: Once your I-140 is approved, ensure your employer files for H-1B extensions beyond the 6-year cap under AC21 Section 104(c) — do not assume HR knows to do this automatically.
heuristics.condition: When an H-1B holder reaches or approaches the standard 6-year cap with an approved I-140 but no current priority date
heuristics.action: Proactively alert your immigration attorney and HR to file for 3-year H-1B extensions under AC21
heuristics.because: Without this extension, status lapses at the 6-year mark — but AC21 explicitly permits indefinite 3-year extensions once I-140 is approved, and many employers miss this trigger

decision_factors.why_it_matters: Per-country quotas (7% cap) create radically different wait times — a qualified Indian EB-2 applicant may wait 50-100+ years while an equally or less qualified applicant from an undersubscribed country may have no wait at all
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Check the USCIS Visa Bulletin Final Action Dates table for your country of birth (not citizenship) in the EB-2 row

decision_factors.why_it_matters: NIW allows self-petition without an employer sponsor, removing PERM dependency and approximately 1 year of processing time — fundamentally changes the strategic pathway
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Assess against three Dhanasar prongs: substantial merit and national importance of work, well-positioned to advance it, and US benefit from waiving labor certification — STEM PhDs with citations/publications are the strongest candidates

decision_factors.why_it_matters: PERM requires employer commitment to a multi-step, ~1-year DOL process including recruitment, prevailing wage filings, and significant legal costs — many small or mid-size employers refuse
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Ask explicitly during offer negotiation whether the company has sponsored PERM before, how many times, and whether they will commit to sponsorship in writing in your offer letter

decision_factors.factor: Current EB-2 vs. EB-3 priority date movement for your country
decision_factors.why_it_matters: For India-born applicants, relative movement between EB-2 and EB-3 in the Visa Bulletin can determine whether a downgrade strategy is worth pursuing — this changes every month
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Monitor the monthly Visa Bulletin Final Action Dates and Dates for Filing charts for both EB-2 and EB-3 for your country of birth; track trajectory over 3-6 months

decision_factors.factor: Evidence strength for NIW or Exceptional Ability category
decision_factors.why_it_matters: NIW approval rate is 54% and declining (down 13 percentage points with increased scrutiny) — weak evidence packages have meaningful denial risk that delays the entire pathway
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Audit against the 6 NIW evidence types: citations/publications, patents, professional association membership, critical role at distinguished organizations, high relative salary, media coverage — a strong case typically has 3+ types with substantial documentation

decision_factors.factor: Time horizon commitment to US residence
decision_factors.why_it_matters: For Indian and Chinese nationals, pursuing a green card is a decade-scale commitment under current rules — this changes whether it makes sense to start the process at all vs. pursuing alternative countries
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Honestly assess whether you are willing and able to remain in the US on H-1B or other non-immigrant status for 10+ years with no guarantee of permanent residence under current policy

decision_factors.why_it_matters: The difference between 15 business days and 8.1 months for I-140 approval can be career-defining — affects job change flexibility, H-1B extension timing, and overall pathway certainty
decision_factors.how_to_assess: Determine whether employer covers the $2,805 premium processing fee or whether you will pay out of pocket; factor this into negotiation with employer

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Starting the EB-2 PERM process with an employer sponsor
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You become implicitly tied to that employer for years — changing jobs resets the PERM clock entirely unless you can port under AC21 after I-140 approval and 180+ days in adjustment of status
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: India-born and China-born professionals who start PERM early in their career before accumulating leverage to negotiate job changes

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Pursuing NIW as a self-petition to avoid employer dependency
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: NIW approval rate is only 54% and declining — a denial means you wasted time, money, and may need to restart with employer-sponsored PERM, losing months or years of priority date advantage
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: STEM professionals who are borderline on evidence strength and self-file without attorney review to save costs

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Remaining on H-1B for years while waiting for an EB-2 priority date to become current
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: You accumulate career and life decisions (home purchases, family planning, entrepreneurship) all contingent on a visa status that can be lost through layoffs, company closures, or policy changes — creating compounding optionality loss
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian nationals in tech who are 5-10 years into the wait and have structured their entire life around an uncertain outcome

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Filing EB-2 instead of EB-3 for India-born applicants
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: In periods where EB-3 India dates advance faster than EB-2 India dates, the 'higher' EB-2 category can paradoxically result in a longer wait than the 'lower' EB-3 category
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: India-born professionals who assume EB-2 is always preferable to EB-3 and don't monitor the Visa Bulletin monthly

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Waiting for standard I-140 processing to save the $2,805 premium processing fee
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: An 8.1-month I-140 delay can cause H-1B cap-gap exposure, delay AC21 portability protections, and prevent the ability to change jobs during a critical career window
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Candidates whose employers decline to pay premium processing fees and who don't understand the downstream cascading effects of the delay

hidden_tradeoffs.choice: Choosing a US career path as an Indian or Chinese national without researching Canada as an alternative
hidden_tradeoffs.unstated_consequence: Canada's Express Entry system can grant permanent residence in months rather than decades for the same qualifications — the opportunity cost of the US path is rarely explicitly compared
hidden_tradeoffs.who_it_affects: Indian STEM professionals who default to the US path due to salary or brand perception without running a parallel Canada assessment

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born applicants file EB-2 without researching their actual priority date wait time, only discovering the 50-100 year backlog years into the process
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students focus on whether they qualify (education, experience) rather than whether the visa will actually be available to them within a career-relevant timeframe — qualifications feel controllable, bureaucratic backlogs feel abstract
failure_patterns.consequence: Years spent in H-1B dependency, constrained job mobility, and deferred life decisions based on a pathway that may never materialize under current law
warning_signs: Student asks 'do I qualify for EB-2?' without also asking 'what is my priority date wait time?'
Student from India or China is planning to file EB-2 but has never looked at the Visa Bulletin

failure_patterns.pattern: Assuming employer will manage the entire green card process and not tracking it personally
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Employees trust HR and immigration counsel to handle compliance — but attorney incentives are aligned with the employer, not the employee, and key deadlines are often missed
failure_patterns.consequence: Missing the H-1B 6-year cap extension window under AC21, failing to preserve priority dates, or losing portability rights through inaction
warning_signs: Employee cannot state their own priority date
Employee does not know whether their I-140 has been approved

failure_patterns.pattern: Filing a NIW petition with weak evidence to avoid employer dependency, resulting in denial
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Students overestimate the strength of their profile based on degree prestige rather than evidence quantity and quality; approval rate declining due to increased USCIS scrutiny
failure_patterns.consequence: Wasted filing fees ($715+), 4+ months of delay, and the need to pivot back to employer-sponsored PERM — losing months of priority date accrual
warning_signs: Applicant has fewer than 3 of the 6 NIW evidence types with substantial documentation
No attorney review of evidence package before filing
Applicant assumes PhD alone is sufficient for NIW approval

failure_patterns.pattern: India-born EB-2 holders never checking whether EB-3 downgrade would accelerate their green card
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: The assumption that a higher preference category is always better is deeply intuitive but factually wrong when per-country retrogression affects EB-2 and EB-3 differently
failure_patterns.consequence: Waiting years longer than necessary when a legal downgrade strategy would have yielded permanent residence sooner
warning_signs: India-born EB-2 holder has never compared their EB-2 and EB-3 India priority dates on the Visa Bulletin
Immigration attorney has not raised the EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade option in consultations

failure_patterns.pattern: Treating the EB-2 pathway as a near-term plan when country of birth makes it a multi-decade commitment
failure_patterns.why_it_happens: Cognitive optimism bias — applicants assume policy will change, that they are special cases, or that wait times will shorten — rather than planning based on current law
failure_patterns.consequence: Career and personal life decisions (job choices, financial planning, family timing, housing) made on false premises about timeline
warning_signs: Indian national says 'I'll have my green card in 5-7 years'
No contingency plan for Canada, return home, or alternative immigration pathways

diagnostic_questions.question: What is your country of birth?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether the EB-2 pathway is viable within a career-relevant timeframe or is effectively unusable under current per-country quota rules
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: If India or China: ask 'Have you checked the current Visa Bulletin Final Action Date for EB-2 for your country, and are you prepared for a potentially multi-decade wait?'
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: If other country: EB-2 is likely current or near-current — focus shifts to PERM vs. NIW track selection and employer sponsorship feasibility

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you have a PhD or equivalent research profile with publications, citations, or patents?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines NIW eligibility — if yes, self-petition may be viable, eliminating employer dependency and PERM timeline entirely
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Assess evidence strength against all 6 NIW evidence types — if 3+ are strongly documentable, NIW is worth pursuing with attorney review
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Pivot to employer-sponsored PERM track — assess employer willingness and whether Exceptional Ability category (3 of 6 evidence types) is achievable

diagnostic_questions.question: Is your employer willing to commit in writing to sponsoring your PERM labor certification?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Determines whether the employer-sponsored EB-2 track is a real option or theoretical — many employers verbally indicate willingness but refuse when the process begins
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Proceed to assess PERM timeline, I-140 processing strategy, and priority date implications for your country
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: NIW self-petition becomes the primary pathway — assess eligibility rigorously; also evaluate Canada as an alternative if NIW is not viable

diagnostic_questions.question: Do you know your current EB-2 priority date and whether it is current in the Visa Bulletin?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Reveals whether the student has basic situational awareness of their own green card status — ignorance here suggests they are not managing their own case
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: For India/China: also ask whether they are tracking EB-3 dates comparatively for downgrade strategy assessment
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Immediate action item: look up the current Visa Bulletin before making any career or immigration decisions

diagnostic_questions.question: Are you willing to remain in the US on non-immigrant status for 10+ years with no guaranteed permanent residence outcome?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: Tests whether the student has honestly internalized the realistic timeline for India/China nationals — if not, alternative pathways (Canada, return home) should be seriously evaluated
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Ensure they have AC21 portability plan, H-1B extension strategy beyond 6-year cap, and contingency plans for layoffs or company closures
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Canada Express Entry, Provincial Nominee Programs, or return-home planning should be explored as higher-probability paths to permanent residence

diagnostic_questions.question: Has an immigration attorney reviewed your NIW evidence package before filing?
diagnostic_questions.what_it_reveals: NIW approval rate is 54% and declining — attorney review is the most reliable predictor of whether the evidence package meets current USCIS scrutiny standards
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_yes: Confirm the attorney assessed against all three Dhanasar prongs, not just evidence quantity
diagnostic_questions.follow_up_if_no: Strong recommendation not to file without review — denial costs 4+ months and filing fees, and a rebuttal or appeal is far more expensive

industry_realities.common_belief: If you are highly qualified, the EB-2 green card process is primarily about documentation and waiting a reasonable amount of time
industry_realities.actual_reality: For India-born applicants, qualifications are essentially irrelevant to wait time — a brilliant researcher from India waits longer than a minimally qualified applicant from an undersubscribed country purely due to the 7% per-country cap
industry_realities.implication: Indian and Chinese nationals should evaluate the US green card pathway based on per-country quota realities first, and qualifications second — the strategic question is not 'can I qualify?' but 'will the visa be available in my lifetime?'

industry_realities.common_belief: NIW is an easy path for STEM PhD holders — having a doctorate from a good university is enough
industry_realities.actual_reality: NIW approval rate has dropped to 54% with increased USCIS scrutiny — a PhD alone is insufficient; the Dhanasar test requires demonstrating national importance of the specific proposed endeavor, not just academic credentials
industry_realities.implication: STEM PhDs pursuing NIW must build a case around the national importance of their specific research area with concrete evidence (citations, patents, peer review, critical organizational role) — not just submit a diploma and CV

industry_realities.common_belief: EB-2 is always a better category than EB-3 because it has a higher preference
industry_realities.actual_reality: For India-born applicants, EB-3 priority dates can advance faster than EB-2 in certain periods due to how retrogression affects each category differently — making a strategic downgrade from EB-2 to EB-3 legally advisable
industry_realities.implication: India-born EB-2 holders must monitor both EB-2 and EB-3 Visa Bulletin dates monthly and consult an attorney when EB-3 dates move ahead of EB-2 dates for their country

industry_realities.common_belief: Once an employer starts your green card process, you're on a clear track and just need to wait
industry_realities.actual_reality: PERM takes ~1 year, I-140 takes 8+ months without premium processing, and the priority date may not become current for decades — each stage has its own failure risks, and employer commitment can evaporate through layoffs, restructuring, or policy changes
industry_realities.implication: Employees must actively manage their own case, know every date and status, and maintain contingency plans rather than passively trusting that the employer's process will deliver permanent residence

industry_realities.common_belief: The EB-2 process costs are primarily the employer's problem
industry_realities.actual_reality: While employers pay PERM and I-140 costs, the employee bears the enormous indirect costs: career mobility constraints, geographic constraints, delayed entrepreneurship, psychological burden of status uncertainty, and the optionality cost of staying when better opportunities exist elsewhere
industry_realities.implication: Students should factor the full cost of employment-based sponsorship — including constrained career mobility — when evaluating whether US green card pursuit makes sense vs. alternative countries where permanent residence is faster

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: How they evaluate the US green card pathway
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Focuses on whether they qualify (degree, years of experience) and assumes that qualifying means the process will complete in a reasonable timeframe
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Immediately checks Visa Bulletin for their country of birth, calculates realistic wait time under current per-country quotas, and only then decides whether to pursue the pathway at all
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Indian and Chinese nationals who skip the priority date check may invest years in a pathway that offers no practical chance of permanent residence under current law

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Defaults to employer-sponsored PERM because it's the 'standard' path and doesn't research self-petition options
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Proactively assesses NIW eligibility against all three Dhanasar prongs before accepting employer dependency, and pursues NIW if evidence is strong enough to survive 54% approval rate scrutiny
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: NIW eliminates a year of PERM process and removes employer dependency — students who never research it forfeit significant strategic optionality

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Managing the EB-2 vs. EB-3 category decision
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes EB-2 is always preferable and never revisits the category choice after filing
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Monitors the Visa Bulletin monthly, maintains awareness of relative EB-2 vs. EB-3 India date movement, and consults an attorney proactively when EB-3 begins advancing faster
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Failing to execute a legal EB-2 to EB-3 downgrade when conditions favor it can cost years of additional wait time that could have been avoided

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Relationship with the green card process
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Delegates entirely to employer HR and immigration counsel, doesn't know their own priority date or I-140 status, and assumes everything is handled
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Maintains their own calendar of all filing dates, priority dates, and deadlines; knows their I-140 approval status and H-1B extension eligibility; treats the attorney as a resource they direct rather than a custodian of their status
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: Immigration attorneys are retained by and primarily accountable to employers — missed deadlines that harm the employee may not be caught unless the employee is actively monitoring their own case

junior_vs_senior_signals.dimension: Long-term country planning for Indian/Chinese nationals
junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Assumes they will get a green card eventually and makes all life decisions (buying a home, family planning, career choices) based on that assumption
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Runs parallel assessment of Canada Express Entry or return-home options alongside US pursuit, maintains optionality, and makes life decisions that remain sound even if the US green card never materializes
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: 50-100 year theoretical wait times under current law mean the US green card may never arrive — students who plan as if it will face compounding life-decision errors over a decade or more

junior_vs_senior_signals.naive_pattern: Accepts standard processing to save the $2,805 fee without understanding the 8-month downstream consequences
junior_vs_senior_signals.informed_pattern: Evaluates the $2,805 premium processing fee against the career and status risks of an 8.1-month I-140 delay — including H-1B extension timing, AC21 portability, and job change flexibility — and advocates for employer coverage of the fee
junior_vs_senior_signals.why_it_matters: An 8-month delay in I-140 approval can cascade into inability to change jobs, H-1B status gaps, and lost portability rights — the fee is small relative to those risks
